docs.title: SSCC 2026 – Quantum Chemistry Hands‑on
docs.text:
This page provides a short orientation and step‑by‑step overview for the SSCC 2026 Quantum Chemistry hands‑on sessions.

All practical work is carried out using Jupyter notebooks in CSC’s computing environment.

docs.title: 1. Access the computing environment
docs.text:
All hands‑on sessions run via CSC Jupyter for Courses using the Mahti web interface.

→ See: environment.md

docs.title: 2. Start with the core session
docs.text:
All participants begin with the core quantum chemistry session, which introduces the basic ORCA workflows used throughout the hands‑on.

This part is common to everyone and provides the foundation for all other sessions.

docs.title: 3. Choose additional sessions
docs.text:
After completing the core session, you may freely choose additional, optional sessions according to your interests, such as:

  • Multireference electronic structure methods
  • Electronic structure and molecular simulation with CP2K

These sessions are independent and can be followed selectively.

→ See: workflow.md

docs.title: 4. Use supporting tools if needed
docs.text:
For building and inspecting molecular structures, an optional graphical editor is available via Mahti Open OnDemand.

→ See: avogadro.md

docs.title: Where to find live information
docs.text:
The course schedule, announcements, and Q&A are handled via the SSCC 2026 HedgeDoc (the course “base camp”):

👉 https://siili.rahtiapp.fi/sscc2026

The notebooks themselves contain the detailed, step‑by‑step instructions for each session.

docs.title: Molecular structure building with Avogadro2
docs.text:
For visual inspection and editing of molecular structures we use Avogadro2 via the Mahti Open OnDemand (OOD) service.

Avogadro2 is used only as a graphical molecule editor. All quantum chemistry calculations are performed in Jupyter notebooks using ORCA or CP2K.

docs.title: Accessing Avogadro2 on Mahti
docs.text:
Avogadro2 is provided via a container installation available in the Mahti OOD environment.

  1. Log in to Mahti Open OnDemand https://www.mahti.csc.fi
  2. Start an Interactive Desktop session
  3. Open a terminal inside the desktop session
  4. Enable and load the Avogadro2 module:
module use /projappl/project_2017403/modules
module load avogadro2

docs.title: Working environment
docs.text:
The SSCC 2026 Quantum Chemistry hands‑on uses CSC Jupyter for Courses, accessed via the Mahti web interface.

All hands‑on work is carried out in this environment. No local installation or software setup is required.

The notebooks rely on:

  • CSC‑provided installations of ORCA and CP2K
  • A custom Python environment prepared for the course

docs.title: Access
docs.text:
• Log in using your CSC or Haka account
  • Ensure multi‑factor authentication (MFA) is activated

docs.title: Starting a notebook session
docs.text:
1. Go to https://www.mahti.csc.fi
  2. Select Jupyter for courses
  3. Choose:
    • Project: project_2017403 (SSCC2026)
    • Course module: select the module corresponding to the session you want to work on
  4. Click Launch

Once the Jupyter environment has started, open the notebooks indicated during the session.

docs.title: During the session
docs.text:
• The notebooks are designed to be largely self‑contained
  • Do not hesitate to ask for help if something is unclear

All required software is preconfigured in the environment.

docs.title: Hands‑on workflow
docs.text:
The SSCC 2026 Quantum Chemistry hands‑on consists of several notebook‑based sessions, each focusing on a different topic or methodology.

This page describes how the sessions fit together conceptually. Detailed, step‑by‑step instructions are provided directly in the notebooks used during each session.

docs.title: Quantum Chemistry hands‑on (Thursday)
docs.text: The Thursday hands‑on sessions focus on electronic structure methods and are organised around a common core session, followed by optional, independent follow‑up sessions.

docs.title: Core quantum chemistry workflows (common to all)
docs.text:
QC engine: ORCA

All participants begin with the core quantum chemistry workflows, based on an introductory ORCA notebook.

After completing the core session, participants will be able to:

  • Understand the key parts of ORCA input files for molecular systems
  • Optimize molecular structures with ORCA
  • Compare total energies of molecular isomers to identify the lowest‑energy structure
  • Calculate and interpret infrared vibrational spectra for molecules

This core session provides the foundation needed for the optional follow‑up sessions.

docs.title: Multireference electronic structure methods (optional)
docs.text:
QC engine: ORCA

The multireference session introduces cases where single‑reference methods are insufficient and multireference approaches are needed.

This session focuses on:

  • Identifying situations that require multireference treatment
  • Understanding the limitations of single‑reference methods
  • Practical multireference calculations using ORCA

Participants may choose this session after completing the core workflows, based on their interests.

docs.title: Electronic structure and molecular simulation with CP2K (optional)
docs.text:
QC engine: CP2K

The CP2K notebooks explore the proton transfer step associated with the interconversion of the two enol forms of 2‑formylcyclohexanone.

The exercises are based on density functional theory (DFT) calculations and illustrate how electronic structure and molecular simulation methods can be used to study reaction pathways and energy barriers.

docs.title: Potential Energy Surface exploration (Friday)
docs.text:
QC engine: ORCA

The Potential Energy Surface (PES) exploration session is held on Friday and consists of a lecture followed by a hands‑on exercise.

This session focuses on understanding and analysing molecular potential energy surfaces using dedicated notebooks and is independent of the Thursday sessions.

docs.title: Relationship between sessions
docs.text:
• The core quantum chemistry workflows are common to all participants
  • Subsequent sessions are optional and can be chosen freely
  • Follow‑up sessions are largely independent, unless stated otherwise
  • Different QC engines (ORCA and CP2K) are used in different sessions
  • All required software is provided via CSC Jupyter for Courses
